title: Ensure multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password

input_row.title: Ensure multi-factor authentication (MFA) is enabled for all IAM users that have a console password
input_row.description: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) adds an extra layer of authentication assurance beyond traditional credentials. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to the AWS Console, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their physical or virtual MFA token. It is recommended that MFA be enabled for all accounts that have a console password.
input_row.rationale: Enabling MFA provides increased security for console access as it requires the authenticating principal to possess a device that displays a time-sensitive key and have knowledge of a credential.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if a MFA device is enabled for all IAM users having a console password: From Console: 1. Open the IAM console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. In the left pane, select Users 3. If the MFA or Password age columns are not visible in the table, click the gear icon at the upper right corner of the table and ensure a checkmark is next to both, then click Close. 4. Ensure that for each user where the Password age column shows a password age, the MFA column shows Virtual, U2F Security Key, or Hardware. From Command Line: 1. Run the following command (macOS/Linux/UNIX) to generate a list of all IAM users along with their password and MFA status: aws iam generate-credential-report aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | cut -d, -f1,4,8 2. The output of this command will produce a table similar to the following: user,password_enabled,mfa_active elise,false,false brandon,true,true rakesh,false,false helene,false,false paras,true,true anitha,false,false 3. For any column having password_enabled set to true , ensure mfa_active is also set to true.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to enable MFA: From Console: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the IAM console at 'https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/' 2. In the left pane, select Users. 3. In the User Name list, choose the name of the intended MFA user. 4. Choose the Security Credentials tab, and then choose Manage MFA Device. 5. In the Manage MFA Device wizard, choose Virtual MFA device, and then choose Continue. IAM generates and displays configuration information for the virtual MFA device, including a QR code graphic. The graphic is a representation of the 'secret configuration key' that is available for manual entry on devices that do not support QR codes. 6. Open your virtual MFA application. (For a list of apps that you can use for hosting virtual MFA devices, see Virtual MFA Applications at https://aws.amazon.com/iam/details/mfa/#Virtual_MFA_Applications). If the virtual MFA application supports multiple accounts (multiple virtual MFA devices), choose the option to create a new account (a new virtual MFA device). 7. Determine whether the MFA app supports QR codes, and then do one of the following: • Use the app to scan the QR code. For example, you might choose the camera icon or choose an option similar to Scan code, and then use the device's camera to scan the code. • In the Manage MFA Device wizard, choose Show secret key for manual configuration, and then type the secret configuration key into your MFA application. When you are finished, the virtual MFA device starts generating one-time passwords. 8. In the Manage MFA Device wizard, in the MFA Code 1 box, type the one-time password that currently appears in the virtual MFA device. Wait up to 30 seconds for the device to generate a new one-time password. Then type the second one-time password into the MFA Code 2 box. 9. Select Assign MFA.
input_row.impact: AWS will soon end support for SMS multi-factor authentication (MFA). New customers are not allowed to use this feature. We recommend that existing customers switch to an alternative method of MFA.
input_row.default_value: By default, IAM users with a console password do not have Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) enabled. MFA must be explicitly configured on each user account.
input_row.additional_information: Forced IAM User Self-Service Remediation Amazon has published a pattern that requires users to set up MFA through self-service before they gain access to their complete set of permissions. Until they complete this step, they cannot access their full permissions. This pattern can be used for new AWS accounts. It can also be applied to existing accounts; it is recommended that users receive instructions and a grace period to complete MFA enrollment before active enforcement on existing AWS accounts.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_user_mfa_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
The automated program would check the Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) status for IAM users who have console passwords. It would perform the following actions:

1. **List IAM Users**: Use `boto3` to call `iam.list_users()` to retrieve all IAM users in the account and store them for subsequent checks.

2. **Generate Credential Report**: Use the AWS CLI command `aws iam generate-credential-report` to create a credential report that includes users and their MFA status.

3. **Retrieve Credential Report**: Subsequently, call `aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text` to download the generated report. The report is base64 encoded, so it would be decoded and parsed.

4. **Parse Output**: After decoding, the output will be in CSV format, with the columns `user`, `password_enabled`, and `mfa_active`. The script would check each user row for the condition:
   - If `password_enabled` is `true`, then check if `mfa_active` is also `true`.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria**:
   - The program will log any users who have `password_enabled` as `true` but `mfa_active` as `false`.
   - Compliance requires that all users with console passwords must also have MFA enabled.

6. **Reporting**: It will generate a report output indicating which users do not comply with the MFA requirement.

By automating these checks, the process becomes efficient and ensures that compliance with the CIS control is maintained consistently across all IAM users.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The task of checking MFA status for IAM users with console passwords can be efficiently automated using AWS APIs, specifically with boto3 and AWS CLI commands.
- AWS provides the necessary API capabilities to perform this task, such as `iam.list_users()` and the credential report commands (`generate-credential-report` and `get-credential-report`).

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of `boto3` to list IAM users and the AWS CLI to generate and retrieve the credential report is valid.
- The program name `aws_iam_user_mfa_enabled` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific to the task, such as indicating the focus on console password users.
- The steps outlined for decoding and parsing the credential report are accurate. However, the explanation could benefit from specifying the handling of base64 decoding more explicitly.
- The pass/fail criteria are correctly defined, focusing on users with `password_enabled` as `true` and `mfa_active` as `false`.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name:** A more descriptive name could be `aws_iam_user_console_password_mfa_check` to clearly indicate the specific security check being performed.
- **Alternative Methods:** Consider using `boto3` for the entire process to maintain consistency, such as using `boto3` for generating and retrieving the credential report instead of mixing with AWS CLI.
- **Error Handling:** The script should include error handling for potential issues, such as failed report generation or retrieval, and handle cases where the credential report might not be immediately available after generation.
- **Frequency of Check:** Ensure that the script accounts for the time it might take for the credential report to be generated and becomes available for retrieval.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-structured and utilizes appropriate AWS services and commands. The automation approach is sound, and the technical details are mostly accurate.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Modify:** Update the program name to `aws_iam_user_console_password_mfa_check` for clarity. Consider using `boto3` exclusively for all operations to maintain consistency and potentially improve error handling and logging.
- The approach and technical details are fundamentally correct, but these improvements can enhance clarity and robustness.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the task of checking MFA status for IAM users with console passwords can be efficiently automated using AWS APIs. The necessary capabilities are provided by AWS through `boto3` and AWS CLI commands, specifically for generating and retrieving credential reports that include MFA status. This allows for a comprehensive and consistent compliance check without manual intervention.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
aws_iam_user_console_password_mfa_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS Resource/Service:** IAM Users
- **API/CLI Method:** 
  - Use `boto3` to call `iam.list_users()` to retrieve all IAM users.
  - Use AWS CLI `aws iam generate-credential-report` to generate the credential report.
  - Use AWS CLI `aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text` to retrieve and decode the report.
- **Properties to Check:** 
  - `password_enabled` and `mfa_active` fields in the credential report.
- **Pass Criteria:** 
  - All users with `password_enabled` as `true` must have `mfa_active` as `true`.
- **Fail Criteria:** 
  - Any user with `password_enabled` as `true` and `mfa_active` as `false` indicates non-compliance.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported the automated approach. The second opinion suggested a more descriptive program name, which has been adopted for clarity.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Amazon_Web_Services_Foundations_Benchmark_v6.0.0
